wafv2.rulegroup.rule.Action.Docs: The action that AWS WAF should take on a web request when it matches the rule's statement. Settings at the web ACL level can override the rule action setting.

wafv2.rulegroup.rule.CaptchaConfig.Docs: Specifies how AWS WAF should handle `CAPTCHA` evaluations. If you don't specify this, AWS WAF uses the `CAPTCHA` configuration that's defined for the web ACL.

wafv2.rulegroup.rule.Name.Docs: The descriptive name of the rule. You can't change the name of a `Rule` after you create it.

wafv2.rulegroup.rule.Priority.Docs: If you define more than one `Rule` in a `WebACL`, AWS WAF evaluates each request against the `Rules` in order based on the value of `Priority`. AWS WAF processes rules with lower priority first. The priorities don't need to be consecutive, but they must all be different.

wafv2.rulegroup.rule.RuleLabels.Docs: Labels to apply to web requests that match the rule match statement. AWS WAF applies fully qualified labels to matching web requests. A fully qualified label is the concatenation of a label namespace and a rule label. The rule's rule group or web ACL defines the label namespace.   Rules that run after this rule in the web ACL can match against these labels using a `LabelMatchStatement`.  For each label, provide a case-sensitive string containing optional namespaces and a label name, according to the following guidelines:  + Separate each component of the label with a colon. + Each namespace or name can have up to 128 characters.+ You can specify up to 5 namespaces in a label.+ Don't use the following reserved words in your label specification: `aws`, `waf`, `managed`, `rulegroup`, `webacl`, `regexpatternset`, or `ipset`.For example, `myLabelName` or `nameSpace1:nameSpace2:myLabelName`.

wafv2.rulegroup.rule.Statement.Docs: The AWS WAF processing statement for the rule, for example ByteMatchStatement or SizeConstraintStatement.

wafv2.rulegroup.rule.VisibilityConfig.Docs: Defines and enables Amazon CloudWatch metrics and web request sample collection.
